revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:跡部の踊り念仏]]
Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu (跡部の踊り念仏, ''Atobe no Odori Nenbutsu'') is a traditional folk performance (''Kyōdo Geinō'') passed down in {{ill|Atobe, Saku, Nagano|ja|跡部 (佐久市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atobe, Saku, Nagano|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Nagano Prefecture<ref>Formerly '''Atobe Village, Minamisaku District''', later part of '''Nozawa Town, Nagano'''.</ref>. Originating from the Odori Nenbutsu (''Dancing Nenbutsu'') of Ippen, the founder of {{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|qid=Q696966|lt=Ji-shu|1=Ji-shu}} during the Kamakura period, it has been designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property<ref name=":1" />, and in 2022, it was inscribed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage<ref name=":0" />. == Overview== It is said that Ippen first performed Odori Nenbutsu in {{ill|Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province|ja|伴野荘 (信濃国佐久郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odagiri-go, Tomono-shō, Shinano Province}} in Saku, and its tradition has been preserved in Atobe District. Originally, the dance was performed outdoors, but today, it takes place inside the main hall of Saifō-ji Temple (''西方寺''), where a "dōjō" (道場, sacred space) is set up. The festival is held annually in April, where men and women perform by beating drums and gongs while chanting Nenbutsu. While Odori Nenbutsu once existed across the Saku region, only the Saifō-ji version has survived<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 893</ref>. == History== It is said that Ippen, upon visiting his uncle Kōno Michisue, who had been exiled to this region, saw a purple cloud and, overwhelmed with awe, began to chant Nenbutsu while dancing. During the Edo period, Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu was performed for three days and nights in the second month of the lunar calendar. However, in the Taishō era, it became part of Saifō-ji’s temple festival. The festival was interrupted during wartime, but was revived in 1952 (Shōwa 27) and moved to April. During the hiatus, many chanting forms (''Rai-go Wasan, Shihō Shōgun, Gokuraku Nenbutsu'') disappeared, leaving only Sai no Kawara Wasan (''Chant of the Riverbed of Sai''). In 2000 (Heisei 12), it was designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/727 |title=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07|qq=}}</ref>, and in November 2022 (Reiwa 4), it was registered as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/bunka/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/atobe-unesco.html |title=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage "Fūryū-odori": Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu |access-date=2024-04-07|qq=}}</ref>. == Performance Details== === Performers=== - The dance is performed by residents of Atobe (''Atobe Kumin''), with no special family lineage or social class requirement. === Stage Setup ("Dōjō")=== - The sacred space (dōjō) consists of a 3.38m-wide platform. * Forty-nine stūpa pillars (塔婆, tōba) are arranged in a 2x2 grid. * Four torii gates represent the Four Gates of Enlightenment: ** East: ''Hossin-mon'' (発心門, Gate of Aspiration) ** South: ''Shugyō-mon'' (修行門, Gate of Practice) ** West: ''Bosatsu-mon'' (菩薩門, Gate of Bodhisattvas) ** North: ''Nehan-mon'' (涅槃門, Gate of Nirvana) * Two drums (taiko) are placed at the center, with prayer beads (Juzu) draped over them. - A canopy (Tengai) with a Phoenix (Hōō) decoration is placed above. - The setup is said to recreate the moment when Ippen and his followers were barred from entering Kamakura and instead performed Odori Nenbutsu at Jizō Hall in Katase. - The decorations (banners, curtains, flowers) are said to resemble the "casket stand" (棺台) used in burials. - Some ritual objects date back to the Edo period and are kept at Saifō-ji. === Costumes=== * Dancers wear striped or kasuri-patterned kimonos and sometimes mourning clothes. * White tabi socks are worn, along with a white collar with "Namu Amida Butsu" inscribed. - Some dancers also wear prayer beads (Juzu). * Drummers wear additional white accessories (''tasuki, tekko, and hachimaki''). === Instruments=== * Drums (''taiko'') were newly crafted in 2006 (Heisei 18), replacing an older drum from 1713 (Shōtoku 3). * Gongs (''kane'') are 16 cm bronze discs, inscribed with "Atobe Village", and believed to have been cast in the Kamakura period at the Atobe Village gold mine site. === Performance Sequence=== * Entry ("Dōjō Iriguchi") *** The drummers signal the start, and the dancers chant the Nembutsu (''Nāmu Amida Butsu''). *** Entering from Nehan-mon, they perform the chanting sequence: ***** Hei-Nenbutsu (''Chanted twice''). ***** Kiri-Nenbutsu (''Final single chant''). ***** Sai no Kawara Wasan (''Chant of the Riverbed of Sai''). *** This first phase ("Ōjō") represents praying for rebirth in the Pure Land. * Dance ("Odori") *** Dancers jump and strike the gongs three times. *** This second phase ("Kansō") represents returning to the world to save others. * Exit ("Dōjō Deguchi") *** Each performer exits one by one from Nehan-mon. - The two-stage structure follows the "Two Types of Transfer of Merit" (''Nishu Ekō'', 二種廻向) doctrine from Hōnen and Shōkū Shōnin.
=== Offering of Dumplings (Dango)=== - After the Odori Nenbutsu, three-colored dango (San-shoku Dango) is offered to the deity. - Eating this dango is said to grant wisdom or prevent colds<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 958</ref>. - Pregnant women eat a larger dango to pray for a safe childbirth. - Since 2024, commercially made dango has been distributed. == Legends and Place Names== * Atobe’s "Butai" district is believed to be the original performance site. * Kanekiba (金鋳場) district is said to be where the gongs were cast. * Ichiba (市庭) district is thought to be where Ippen first danced. === The "Purple Cloud" Legend=== - According to tradition, Ippen was inspired to dance upon seeing a purple cloud. - About 300m south of Atobe, there is a Jishū temple, Kintai-ji, whose mountain name is "Shiun-zan" (Purple Cloud Mountain)<ref>Saku City (1990), p. 1627</ref>. == References== {{Reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading== * Saku City (1990), ''Saku City History – Folklore Edition, Volume 2'', Saku City History Publication Committee. == External Links== - [https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kanko/spot/meisho_shiseki/atobe.html Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu – Saku City Official Site] - [http://www.82bunka.or.jp/bunkazai/legend/detail/04/post-19.php Atobe’s Odori Nenbutsu – Shinshū Cultural Foundation] '' {{ill|Wago Nembutsu Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wago Nembutsu Odori|qq=}} '' {{ill|Niino Bon Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niino Bon Odori|qq=}} ; {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|qid=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}} '' {{ill|Gujo Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gujo Odori|qq=}} '' {{ill|Kanmizu no Kake Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanmizu no Kake Odori|qq=}} ; {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} '' {{ill|Tokuyama Bon Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokuyama Bon Odori|qq=}} '' {{ill|Utomaki Bon Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utomaki Bon Odori|qq=}} ; {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}} '' {{ill|Ayato Night Nembutsu and Bon Odori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ayato Night Nembutsu and Bon Odori|qq=}} ; {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} '' {{ill|Katte Shrine Ritual Dances|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katte Shrine Ritual Dances|qq=}}
